raw_output:
{
  "risk_tier": "High",
  "risk_summary": "This deployment processes biometric facial data to make or substantially influence employment decisions, triggering the highest-risk obligations under the EU AI Act, GDPR, Illinois BIPA, California privacy law, and New York City automated employment decision tools law, with significant exposure across multiple jurisdictions.",
  "applicable_frameworks": [
    {
      "name": "Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "BIPA",
      "jurisdiction": "US State – Illinois",
      "why_applicable": "The system performs facial expression analysis generating biometric identifiers and biometric information from video interviews, directly triggering BIPA's collection, retention, and consent requirements even if the company targets NY and CA firms, as Illinois residents may be candidates."
    },
    {
      "name": "General Data Protection Regulation",
      "abbr": "GDPR",
      "jurisdiction": "European Union",
      "why_applicable": "The platform is expanding to the EU in Q3 and processes biometric data (special category under Article 9) and makes automated decisions substantially affecting employment, triggering Articles 9, 22, and associated obligations."
    },
    {
      "name": "EU Artificial Intelligence Act",
      "abbr": "EU AI Act",
      "jurisdiction": "European Union",
      "why_applicable": "AI systems used for recruitment, candidate screening, and assessment of persons in employment contexts are explicitly classified as high-risk under Annex III, and emotion recognition systems face additional restrictions under Article 50."
    },
    {
      "name": "California Consumer Privacy Act / California Privacy Rights Act",
      "abbr": "CCPA/CPRA",
      "jurisdiction": "US State – California",
      "why_applicable": "The platform targets California companies and processes biometric information (sensitive personal information under CPRA Section 1798.140), requiring specific opt-in consent and limiting use to disclosed purposes."
    },
    {
      "name": "California CPPA Automated Decisionmaking Technology Regulations",
      "abbr": "CA ADMT Regs",
      "jurisdiction": "US State – California",
      "why_applicable": "The system uses automated decisionmaking technology to evaluate candidates for employment, triggering California's forthcoming ADMT regulations requiring opt-out rights and pre-use notices."
    },
    {
      "name": "New York City Local Law 144 (Automated Employment Decision Tools)",
      "abbr": "NYC Local Law 144",
      "jurisdiction": "US – New York City",
      "why_applicable": "The platform targets New York companies and uses an automated employment decision tool to screen or evaluate candidates, directly triggering NYC's bias audit and notice requirements."
    },
    {
      "name": "New Jersey Data Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "NJDPA",
      "jurisdiction": "US State – New Jersey",
      "why_applicable": "NJDPA explicitly includes facial mapping, facial geometry, and facial templates in its definition of biometric data and covers sensitive data processing with consent requirements applicable to candidates who may be NJ residents."
    },
    {
      "name": "Minnesota Consumer Data Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "MN MCDPA",
      "jurisdiction": "US State – Minnesota",
      "why_applicable": "Processing biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying an individual constitutes sensitive data under MN MCDPA Section 325M.11, requiring consent and data protection assessments for profiling used in employment decisions."
    },
    {
      "name": "Oregon Consumer Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "OCPA",
      "jurisdiction": "US State – Oregon",
      "why_applicable": "OCPA classifies biometric data and profiling used to make employment decisions as sensitive data processing requiring consent and data protection assessments, applicable to Oregon-resident candidates."
    }
  ],
  "gaps": [
    {
      "title": "No BIPA-compliant written policy, consent, or retention schedule for biometric data",
      "regulation": "Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "BIPA",
      "article": "740 ILCS 14/15(a), 14/15(b)",
      "description": "BIPA Section 15(a) requires a publicly available written retention and destruction policy for biometric identifiers. Section 15(b) requires informed written consent before collecting biometric identifiers or information. The deployment collects facial geometry and expression data from video interviews without evidence of BIPA-compliant written consent, a published retention schedule, or a destruction policy. Statutory damages of $1,000–$5,000 per violation apply.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Immediately enforceable – BIPA has been in effect since 2008"
    },
    {
      "title": "BIPA prohibition on profiting from or disclosing biometric data without consent",
      "regulation": "Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "BIPA",
      "article": "740 ILCS 14/15(c), 14/15(d)",
      "description": "BIPA Section 15(c) prohibits selling, leasing, trading, or profiting from biometric data. Section 15(d) prohibits disclosing biometric data to third parties without consent. The platform's SaaS model likely transmits biometric scores to client employers, which constitutes disclosure requiring separate written consent and potentially triggers the profit prohibition.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Immediately enforceable"
    },
    {
      "title": "Processing special category biometric data without a valid GDPR Article 9 basis",
      "regulation": "General Data Protection Regulation",
      "abbr": "GDPR",
      "article": "Article 9(1), Article 9(2)",
      "description": "Biometric data processed for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person is special category data under Article 9(1). The deployment has no identified Article 9(2) exemption (e.g., explicit consent under 9(2)(a) or a Member State law basis). Emotion detection data may also constitute data concerning health under Article 9(1). Processing must cease or a lawful Article 9(2) basis must be established before EU expansion.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Before Q3 EU expansion"
    },
    {
      "title": "No GDPR Article 22 safeguards for solely automated employment decisions",
      "regulation": "General Data Protection Regulation",
      "abbr": "GDPR",
      "article": "Article 22(1), Article 22(3)",
      "description": "Article 22(1) prohibits solely automated decisions that produce legal or similarly significant effects (including employment decisions) without an Article 22(2) basis. Even where a basis exists, Article 22(3) requires the controller to implement suitable measures including the right to obtain human review, express their point of view, and contest the decision. The deployment as described substantially influences hiring decisions with no described human override or contestation mechanism.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Before Q3 EU expansion"
    },
    {
      "title": "No GDPR Article 13/14 transparency disclosure for automated profiling and biometric processing",
      "regulation": "General Data Protection Regulation",
      "abbr": "GDPR",
      "article": "Article 13(2)(f), Article 14(2)(g)",
      "description": "Controllers must inform data subjects about the existence of automated decision-making including profiling, meaningful information about the logic involved, and the significance and envisaged consequences. The deployment uses opaque AI scoring of emotion and facial expressions with no described candidate-facing transparency notice meeting these requirements.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Before Q3 EU expansion"
    },
    {
      "title": "EU AI Act high-risk classification: missing conformity obligations for employment AI",
      "regulation": "EU Artificial Intelligence Act",
      "abbr": "EU AI Act",
      "article": "Article 6, Annex III(4), Article 9, Article 13, Article 14, Article 26",
      "description": "AI systems used for recruitment or selection of natural persons, including screening or filtering applications and evaluating candidates in interviews, are classified as high-risk under Annex III, point 4. This triggers mandatory obligations: a quality management system (Article 9), technical documentation, logging and traceability, transparency to deployers (Article 13), human oversight measures (Article 14), accuracy and robustness requirements (Article 15), and conformity assessment before EU market placement. None of these are described as in place.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "EU AI Act high-risk provisions apply from August 2026; GPAI and prohibited practices from August 2024/February 2025"
    },
    {
      "title": "EU AI Act: emotion recognition in workplace/recruitment context may be prohibited or restricted",
      "regulation": "EU Artificial Intelligence Act",
      "abbr": "EU AI Act",
      "article": "Article 50(1), Article 5(1)(f) (as applicable)",
      "description": "Article 50(1) requires providers of emotion recognition systems to inform natural persons when they are subject to such systems. More critically, Article 5(1)(f) prohibits placing on the market AI systems that infer emotions of natural persons in workplace and educational institution contexts, except for safety reasons. The deployment explicitly uses emotion detection in a hiring/employment context, which may constitute a prohibited practice in the EU.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Prohibition on unacceptable risk AI practices applies from February 2025"
    },
    {
      "title": "NYC Local Law 144: no bias audit or candidate notice for automated employment decision tool",
      "regulation": "New York City Local Law 144",
      "abbr": "NYC Local Law 144",
      "article": "NYC Admin. Code § 20-871, § 20-872",
      "description": "NYC Local Law 144 requires employers and employment agencies using automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) to conduct independent bias audits before use and publish audit results. Candidates must be notified at least 10 business days before use of the AEDT, including what data is collected. The deployment targets New York companies with no described bias audit or candidate notice programme.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "In effect since July 5, 2023"
    },
    {
      "title": "CCPA/CPRA: sensitive personal information (biometric) processing without required opt-in consent",
      "regulation": "California Consumer Privacy Act / California Privacy Rights Act",
      "abbr": "CCPA/CPRA",
      "article": "Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.121, § 1798.140(ae)(1)(B)",
      "description": "Biometric information is sensitive personal information under CPRA Section 1798.140(ae)(1)(B). Section 1798.121 grants consumers the right to limit use of sensitive personal information. The deployment collects and uses biometric facial data from candidates at California employers without described opt-in consent or a mechanism to limit such use.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "CPRA in effect since January 1, 2023"
    },
    {
      "title": "NJDPA: biometric data (facial mapping/geometry) collected without sensitive data consent",
      "regulation": "New Jersey Data Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "NJDPA",
      "article": "C.56:8-166.4 (definitions – biometric data; sensitive data), Section 6 (consent for sensitive data)",
      "description": "NJDPA explicitly includes facial mapping, facial geometry, and facial templates in its biometric data definition, and classifies biometric data processing as sensitive data requiring opt-in consent. The deployment processes this data in a hiring context affecting NJ-resident candidates without described consent mechanisms meeting NJDPA's freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous standard.",
      "severity": "Medium",
      "deadline": "Effective January 15, 2025"
    },
    {
      "title": "GDPR: no Data Protection Impact Assessment for high-risk biometric processing",
      "regulation": "General Data Protection Regulation",
      "abbr": "GDPR",
      "article": "Article 35(1), Article 35(3)(a), Article 35(3)(b)",
      "description": "Article 35(3) mandates a DPIA prior to processing that involves systematic and extensive profiling producing legal or similarly significant effects, or large-scale processing of special category data. This deployment falls squarely within both categories. No DPIA is described.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Before Q3 EU expansion"
    },
    {
      "title": "MN MCDPA: biometric sensitive data processing without consent and data protection assessment",
      "regulation": "Minnesota Consumer Data Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "MN MCDPA",
      "article": "325M.11 (sensitive data definition), 325M.13 (consent), 325M.15 (data protection assessments)",
      "description": "MN MCDPA classifies processing of biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying an individual as sensitive data requiring opt-in consent. Profiling that produces decisions with legal or similarly significant effects (employment) also requires a data protection assessment. The deployment lacks described consent workflows or assessments for Minnesota-resident candidates.",
      "severity": "Medium",
      "deadline": "Effective July 31, 2025"
    },
    {
      "title": "OCPA: biometric data processing without consent and data protection assessment retention",
      "regulation": "Oregon Consumer Privacy Act",
      "abbr": "OCPA",
      "article": "ORS 646A.570(3), ORS 646A.576, ORS 646A.585(6)",
      "description": "OCPA defines biometric data as sensitive data and requires consent for its processing. For profiling with significant effects on employment, a data protection assessment must be conducted and retained for at least five years (ORS 646A.585(6)). No consent mechanism or assessment retention programme is described.",
      "severity": "Medium",
      "deadline": "Effective July 1, 2024"
    },
    {
      "title": "Absence of cross-jurisdictional candidate consent framework for biometric data collection",
      "regulation": "Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act / General Data Protection Regulation / CCPA/CPRA",
      "abbr": "BIPA / GDPR / CCPA/CPRA",
      "article": "BIPA 740 ILCS 14/15(b); GDPR Article 7; CCPA/CPRA § 1798.121",
      "description": "Each jurisdiction imposes distinct, non-interchangeable consent standards for biometric and sensitive data. BIPA requires prior written consent before collection; GDPR requires explicit, freely given, specific consent for special category data; CPRA requires a right to limit sensitive data use. No unified, jurisdiction-aware consent management framework is described, creating systemic compliance failure across all markets.",
      "severity": "High",
      "deadline": "Immediate (BIPA, CCPA/CPRA, NYC LL144 are currently enforceable)"
    }
  ],
  "recommended_actions": [
    {
      "priority": 1,
      "action": "Conduct an immediate legal review to determine whether EU AI Act Article 5(1)(f) prohibits the emotion recognition component in employment contexts; if prohibited, redesign or remove emotion detection before any EU deployment and evaluate whether the system can lawfully operate in the EU at all without fundamental redesign.",
      "regulation": "EU Artificial Intelligence Act",
      "timeframe": "immediate"
    },
    {
      "priority": 2,
      "action": "Implement BIPA-compliant written informed consent forms, publish a biometric data retention and destruction schedule on your website, and establish a destruction protocol for biometric data no later than the earlier of 3 years or when the purpose is fulfilled. Extend these controls to cover CCPA/CPRA sensitive data opt-in and NYC Local Law 144 10-business-day advance notice to candidates.",
      "regulation": "Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act / CCPA/CPRA / NYC Local Law 144",
      "timeframe": "immediate"
    },
    {
      "priority": 3,
      "action": "Commission an independent bias audit of the automated employment decision tool meeting NYC Local Law 144 requirements and publish the results; establish an ongoing annual audit cadence and ensure candidate-facing disclosures are in place for all
